1: A type: “Take a clay tile and place it before you, then inscribe on it the city — Jerusalem.” The enacted prophecy of the siege tile is Ezekiel’s most dramatic prophetic sign-act. The inscribed city under siege is a type of Christ’s weeping over Jerusalem: “O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... your enemies will set up a barricade around you” (Luke 19:41-44). Both the prophet’s tile and the Savior’s tears represent the same approaching reality — the siege and fall of the city that killed the prophets.
2: A structural note: The full siege apparatus inscribed or enacted around the clay tile — siege wall, ramp, encampments, battering rams — enacts the comprehensive nature of coming judgment. The precision of the enumeration (four siege elements) mirrors the precision of Babylon’s actual siege of Jerusalem in 588-586 BCE. Jesus’s description of Jerusalem’s siege in Luke 21:20-24 is equally precise: “when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, know that its desolation has come near.”
3: A type: “Take an iron griddle and stand it upright as an iron wall between yourself and the city.” The iron wall between Ezekiel and the siege-city represents YHWH’s judgment standing between him and Jerusalem — the divine presence as an impassable barrier of wrath. The same imagery appears in Lamentations 3:44 (“you have wrapped yourself with a cloud so that no prayer can pass through”). Christ on the cross cries “my God, my God, why have you forsaken me” (Matthew 27:46) — the iron wall of divine judgment between Father and Son in the moment of atonement.
4: A type: “Lie on your left side and take up the iniquity of the house of Israel upon yourself.” This is the most explicit substitutionary bearing of sin in Ezekiel — the prophet physically enacts the taking of communal iniquity upon his own body. The language and structure directly anticipate Isaiah 53:4-6 (“he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows... the chastisement of our peace was upon him”). Christ’s bearing of sin on the cross is the ultimate fulfillment of Ezekiel’s enacted bearing: where Ezekiel bore it symbolically for days, Christ bore it actually and once for all.
5: A structural note: The 390 days representing the years of Israel’s iniquity have been calculated in various ways by interpreters (some find 390 years from the division of the kingdom to the fall of Jerusalem; others calculate from earlier reference points). Regardless of the precise calculation, the correspondence of days to years establishes the principle that prophetic time is measured differently from calendar time — the temporal density of sin and judgment does not fit ordinary chronology. Paul’s similar compaction of the “fullness of time” when Christ was born (Galatians 4:4) reflects this prophetic-time sensibility.
6: A type: “When those days are finished, roll onto your right side and bear the iniquity of the house of Judah: forty days, a day for each year.” The 40 days of bearing Judah’s iniquity connects to the OT’s several 40-day/40-year periods of testing and preparation: the flood, the wilderness, Moses on Sinai. Jesus fasts 40 days in the wilderness before his ministry (Matthew 4:2), bearing in his own person the wilderness testing that Israel failed. The 40-day pattern is the preparation-through-bearing that leads to mission.
7: A structural note: “Turn your face toward the besieged Jerusalem with your arm bared, and prophesy against her.” The bared arm (zeroa choluf) is the gesture of military action — YHWH acts against Jerusalem with his own arm. The same image appears in the exodus (“by a mighty hand and an outstretched arm,” Deuteronomy 26:8) but here reversed: the arm that delivered Israel from Egypt now acts in judgment against Israel. Christ’s lament over Jerusalem (“would that you had known the things that make for peace,” Luke 19:42) is the fully personal counterpart of Ezekiel’s directed prophecy.
8: A structural note: The cords binding Ezekiel to his side for the duration of the sign-act create a physical compulsion that mirrors the compulsion of the prophet’s call. Jeremiah describes the same compulsion: “if I say I will not speak any more in his name, then within me it is like a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I am weary of holding it back, and I cannot” (Jeremiah 20:9). Christ’s willingness to be bound at Gethsemane (“all this has taken place that the Scriptures might be fulfilled,” Matthew 26:56) is the ultimate acceptance of the binding that accomplishes the divine purpose.
9: A type: The multi-grain siege bread — wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, emmer — eaten during the siege-sign enacts the famine conditions of a besieged city. The mixing of five grains that would not normally be combined represents desperation and scarcity. Jesus feeds the 5,000 from five loaves (a different kind of five-grain provision) — not scarcity but superabundance; not the bread of judgment but the bread of grace. Christ is the bread of life that replaces the famine bread: “whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35).
10: A structural note: The rationed food (twenty shekels by weight per day) and the measured water (one-sixth of a hin) establish the conditions of extreme scarcity that the besieged city will experience. In contrast, Christ at the wedding of Cana provides 120-180 gallons of wine (John 2:6) — superabundance where scarcity was expected. The contrast between Ezekiel’s rationed bread and Christ’s abundant provision is the contrast between judgment and grace.
11: A structural note: The measured water — a sixth of a hin drunk at set times — continues the rationing image. Isaiah’s vision of eschatological blessing includes “streams in the desert” (Isaiah 35:6) and Ezekiel’s own vision includes the rivers of living water flowing from the temple (Ezek 47). Christ promises “rivers of living water will flow from within him” (John 7:38) — the measured water of judgment is replaced by the overflowing water of life.
12: A type: The command to bake barley bread over human excrement — maximum ritual defilement — is one of the most extreme prophetic sign-acts in the OT. The defilement of the food supply represents the ultimate covenantal degradation: eating what is ritually impure under duress, because no other fuel is available. Christ “became sin for us” (2 Corinthians 5:21) — entering into the maximum degradation on behalf of those who had corrupted themselves. Where Ezekiel protests the defilement (Ezekiel 4:14), Christ accepted it voluntarily.
13: A structural note: “This is how the Israelites will eat their defiled bread among the nations to which I drive them.” The exile as defiled-bread eating among the nations is the condition from which Christ’s redemption liberates: the Gentile mission (Acts 10-11) specifically overturns the clean/unclean food barriers as a sign that the exile-condition of defilement among the nations has been ended in Christ. Peter’s vision of the unclean animals (Acts 10:15: “what God has made clean, do not call common”) is the direct reversal of Ezekiel’s defiled-bread oracle.
14: A pattern: “Ah, Lord GOD! I have never defiled myself.” Ezekiel’s priestly protest against eating defiled bread — his lifelong ritual purity — is the prophetic analog of Christ’s sinlessness. Christ, the great High Priest, is “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26) — he had never defiled himself. Yet he accepted the defilement of sin imputed to him on the cross, just as Ezekiel was granted the concession of cattle dung rather than human excrement — but with Christ there is no concession, no substitute: he took the full defilement.
15: A structural note: YHWH’s concession — cattle dung instead of human excrement — reflects the mercy that modifies but does not cancel judgment. Ezekiel’s priestly integrity is accommodated within the sign-act. In Christ, there is no such moderation of the defilement accepted: he drank the cup of divine judgment to the dregs (Mark 14:36: “remove this cup from me... yet not what I will, but what you will”), where Ezekiel was permitted a lighter version of the symbol.
16: A type: “Son of man, I am about to break the food supply in Jerusalem; they will eat their rationed bread with dread and drink their measured water with despair.” The breaking of the food supply is the direct reversal of the covenant blessing (“you shall eat your bread to the full, and I will give peace in your land,” Leviticus 26:5). Christ at the Last Supper breaks bread and pours wine — reversing the broken/measured imagery: “this is my body, broken for you... this cup is the new covenant in my blood” (1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The bread and wine of blessing replace the rationed bread and water of judgment.
17: A structural note: “They will lack bread and water, look at each other in horror, and waste away because of their iniquity.” The wasting away of covenant breakers — not from famine alone but from the moral horror of recognizing what their iniquity has produced — is the condition from which Christ’s gospel delivers. The prodigal son “was perishing with hunger” in the far country (Luke 15:17) and “came to himself” — the same recognition-of-iniquity moment that precedes return. Christ provides what satisfies the wasting — “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35).
